peer_exemplars.reasoning: High libertarianism and anti-authoritarianism drive support for participatory democracy, but low openness and conventional moral stage temper enthusiasm.
Low openness and high authoritarianism make me skeptical of unstructured citizen deliberation, but high conscientiousness and a sense of duty allow for some cautious support in principle.
Moderate support from high GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference (64.58) and free-spirit autonomy (66.71), tempered by very low agreeableness (16.2) and skepticism of collective deliberation.

Low openness and very low agreeableness, combined with high authoritarianism and a preference for strong leadership, make this person skeptical of participatory citizen assemblies that could challenge established authority.

High openness to experience and a preference for participatory democracy (GAL-TAN participatory democracy at 39.24) combine with a strong system-justification orientation (92.22) to produce moderate support for citizen assemblies as a deliberative complement to existing institutions.

Very low openness and low GAL-TAN green-libertarian scores (3.84%) indicate deep skepticism toward participatory democratic innovations, while high authoritarian submission (71.55%) and conventionalism (84.84%) favor established hierarchical decision-making over citizen-led assemblies.

High openness and a strong GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference (46.17) combine with a libertarian skepticism of state control (14.75) to produce moderate support for citizen assemblies as a check on elite power.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong fairness and anti-dominance values, combined with low authoritarian aggression, support loser consent, though high system justification slightly reduces intensity.

Weak support due to low political trust (external efficacy 18.45) and high system justification (92.88) creating a grudging acceptance of procedural norms despite low faith in fairness.

High conventionalism and system justification support a normative commitment to democratic procedures, but very low agreeableness and high collective narcissism temper full endorsement of losing gracefully.

Extremely high political trust (97.99), very high system justification (92.22), and strong conventionalism (99.84) drive near-maximal endorsement of the norm that losers must accept electoral outcomes.

High authoritarian submission (71.55%) and conventionalism (84.84%) support orderly acceptance of electoral outcomes, but low trust in government (8.95%) and low external political efficacy (60.62%) temper this into moderate rather than strong endorsement.
High political trust (84.84) and civic nationalism (90.06) support a firm commitment to democratic norms, though a moderate authoritarian submission (28.81) tempers the score slightly.

Low trust in government (8.95%) and low political system justification (37.76%) create skepticism about electoral integrity, though high conventionalism (84.84%) provides some baseline trust in procedures.

Very high trust in government (84.84) and high institutional legitimacy (80.34) drive trust in the electoral process, tempered slightly by low internal efficacy (19.84) and a moderate populist orientation.
